speaker: interviewer
text: so they'll be out like a minute or so break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but, how do we start? How do we start?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going to start, I'm going to ask you your name--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. --where you were-- you're going to tell me where you were born. I mean, you-- response to your questions?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning, but then if you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, only the beginning. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll see. It'll just kind of flow along. I'll give you a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, that's what I want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a little zest, a little boost.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do we start?

speaker: interviewer
text: Me, I'll start.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I wanted to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You start by giving your name, where you were born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, I just wanted to know how we start.

speaker: interviewer
text: You feel comfortable saying when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no no I mean I start answering your question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Just what I wanted to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to put this in front of your face in a minute, just for a couple seconds. Just like this, just for about five, six, seven seconds, just so you know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's April 2nd, 1995. Survivor is Gregory Leyfell. The interviewer is Pamela Goodman. The city is Jamaica, United States of America, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's April 2, 1995. We're in Jamaica, New York, interviewing Gregory Leyfell . The interviewer is Pamela Goodman, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pamela, can you spell your name please. G-O-O-D-M-A-N is the last name. First name is Pamela, P-A-M-E-L-A. Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to begin by giving us your name, where you were born, the year you were born, please. You can begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am I Gregory Leyfell. I-- the name Gregory I got here in the United States because they had difficulty getting people to pronounce it, my original name, in English. So I am now known as Gregory Leyfell dash Grisha Leyfell. I was born April 27, 1906 in the city Ostrog. At that time, it was part of Russia. Later, this town in the 1920s, it, it became part of Poland. I went to school in, in Ostrog, the gymnasium at that time. My father was a businessman. He had a, a textile business, a retail, rather. And later on, after they closed the gymnasium because it was-- the gymnasium was in Russian. And they switched, and said everybody had to learn Polish. And I had to, to look for work. And since my father had connections in business with textile, I went to and got a job in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at that time 20-something, 20. No, originally, I started earlier. Started I was about 19 years old. 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back for a minute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had two sisters and three brothers. Unfortunately, the only survivors is I, myself, survived under the Nazi occupation. And my bro-- brother, who was younger, evacuated and survived in, in Asia, and Samarkand, and other Russian cities. And he came back to Poland after repatriation, repatriation. In the mean time in 1939, when the Germans started the war with Portland, I was living in Lodz at that time. And we ran away right away, before the Germans came to Lodz-- my, my brother, and I, some other friends on foot. And we came to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest of the family was in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were there. My, my family was in Ostrog, but this was-- but I was in Lodz at that time. So we ran away on foot and the Germans were shooting at us all the way to Warsaw. And in War-- Warsaw was already encircled by German-- by the German army. So we got stuck, and we survived the-- a whole month, the seige of Warsaw. The siege of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1939. 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that we decided to go east to avoid to be occupied by Germans. But the Germans came in to Warsaw and we went farther to the east. And we came-- I came-- we managed to come to my city where I was born, to Ostrog. And at that time this part of Poland was occupied by the Russian army-- by the Russian army. I spent little time in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were conditions like there, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was occupied by the Russians, and they didn't do war. They didn't have the war there because the Russia and Germany had a pact. They divided Poland, you know, and this was part of Russia. Fortunately for me because in this part of Poland, as it was called Kresy Wschodnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew Russian, the Russian language. I knew the Russian very well because I got my education in Russian. So I left Ostrog to, to the larger city, to Lvov, or as they call it in German, Lemberg. And I found work there. I found work there, and I-- What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liquor. Liquor. I found my job there thanks to the fact that I knew Russian. Because some of these occupants, the Russians-- and this was Ukraine-- as they did not know Polish, and they didn't know Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did director of a factory of liquor. I found out that the director is looking for somebody with the knowledge of Russian, and he was looking for a secretary-- a girl secretary. But I found out about that, and I came to him. He was very disappointed that not a girl came ask for the job. But since he needed somebody, he couldn't function somehow without, so he accepted me as the secretary. And that's at the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: When in Lemberg, well, we found-- my brother also came to Lemberg. And he worked also somewhere on the railroad office or something. But they-- the Russian authorities, the Soviet authorities, started to-- all these people who came from Poland, all this, as they're called, the refugees. The refugees, they took, or started to take them away, all the way into deep Siberia as for work, you know, for work. And anybody who, who couldn't prove that he lived there before the war was taken by force and taken away all the way to Siberia. And then, because I did know the Russian language, somehow I managed to persuade them and convince them-- then convince them that I lived there before, and I got a passport, the passport. Anybody-- any of the refugees who did not have a passport, a Soviet passport already was taken away by force and had to-- back to the Soviet Union. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So, it was-- this was in 1939, 1940, and 1941. In 1941, I got a, like a vacation. And I went to a place, Yaremche, the Carpathian mountains. And in June-- I remember the day-- we heard over the radio this speech of Molotov, the Russian minister of foreign affairs, that the Germans attacked Russia. So, we have started a new-- a new face so it was a new-- a new war. I didn't know what to do with myself. So I was not in Lemberg, and my brother was in Lemberg. So I tried to-- I tried to get back to Lemberg, and join my brother to evacuate somewhere together. But when I came to Lemberg, my brother was left already somehow. I didn't know how-- I didn't-- but he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to get away too with a-- with a group of friends from that I knew from, from Lodz. And we are supposed to leave, and the authorities all promised now a truck to take us away, to, to Slovakia. But we came to the spot where the truck was supposed to be, but unfortunately there was no truck. And anybody who wanted to go away had to go on, on feet. The situation, the, the mood among the Ukrainian population was very anti-Semitic and anti-Soviet. And I was afraid to that on the way I'll be killed just, just being a Jew or just running away. Because the Ukrainian population was sympathizing with the-- with the Germans. They are [INAUDIBLE]. So unfortunately, I made the wrong decision, and I remained. I remained in Lemberg, and it was the wrong decision. But, it was like-- and then there I met one who originally came from my-- from the place where I was born. And we decided to-- he decided, and I decided to, to go away. And then a group of people who originally came from a town Kremenets, Krzemieniec in Polish. And they ran away from the-- they were afraid of the Soviets. They were the richer people, and some of the richer people were taken away by the Soviets authorities. So they went to, to Lvov, to Lemberg. But now, they decided to go back to their-- to their town, to, to Kremenets. And I found-- my friend found out about it, and we decided to join them. They still had-- they had money, and they hired a truck with two German soldiers as guards. They were paid, the Germans. So they went to-- they took us to Kremenets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these friends Jewish? These friends of yours, were they Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. These were mostly Jewish people who were-- they were afraid because they were rich, relatively rich. So they-- they are afraid they were taking away. But now they thought-- they didn't realize all the situation. Anyway, they decided to go back to Kremenets, and I joined there, too, because I had my oldest brother lived in Kremenets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Kremenets, and it was the evening. And they told us on the same day, the Germans took away a few thousands of men, especially men, and took them away and were shot. So I've somehow managed to get to the place where my brother lived with his wife. They didn't have children. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother had a little grocery store-- before, I mean. And one day a lady, a Russian lady, Russian-- her background was rather the Russian aristocracy, the Russian aristocracy. But she used to be a customer of my brother. And she used to buy groceries there. So she came and asked, maybe they could give-- sell her or give her soap, soap.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, the Jewish people were not allowed to go to the market to buy-- to, to, to do anything. And she said, so my sister-in-law told her she can give her a piece of soap, but maybe she could-- money was of no use. Maybe she could give her a piece of butter or something like this. So this Russian lady said yes, she could do, but maybe somebody would go with her to her home, to take this. And then my sister-in-law asked me maybe I could do it. And the Jewish people at that time already had the-- sorry, yellow patches like this. So I decided to go, and I covered with a rain coat this-- Yellow star.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yellow patch. And I went with her to her home to get this piece of butter. Well, as I mentioned before, I knew the language very well. I also knew I am a product of the Russian culture. I knew lots of Russian literature, Russian music.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow the, the lady, who was Russian, somehow got very sympathetic to me. And she said-- she offered any time I want to come-- the Jews are not supposed-- not allowed to visit the Gentiles. But told me that any time I can, I have the chance, I could come to her house, you know? So of course, I, whenever I could, I, I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the same house, one apartment-- she lived in one apartment on the main floor. And on across the aisle was the owner of the house, also a Russian lady. Her name was Maria Nikitichna, Nikitichna. And also somehow she, she liked me-- how to say it-- liked me. How she was very sympathetic, very understanding to the situation and tried to, to show it any way she could. And also offered me food and said anytime way I can come, I should come to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the situation-- and they created a ghetto, a ghetto. And the situation was worse and worse. Tell us about conditions in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the conditions of the ghetto are, are disastrous. First of all, there was no food. There was hunger. And secondly, they-- nobody knew what can happen to him today or tomorrow, you know? The rumors started, they liquidated the ghetto in another town, another city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get communications? How did you hear about things?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only by rumors, only by rumors. Only by from, from the outside, you know? These people, the Ukrainian, the Gen-- they could travel, and they brought news, information about that. But the situation was very, very, very bad. And I remember one day I met near-- there was like a university, a [? liceum. ?] The name of Polish was [POLISH], [? liceum. ?] And I met this Maria Nikitichna, this Russian woman, and she said-- she told me there are rumors that something very bad is going to happen to the Jewish population. And I told her, and I have a joking way, because I was so desperate, you know, so that I said to her, you know there is a saying in the Polish language. Whatever happens to the Jews might as well happen to me. And then she said, no, you shouldn't say it. We should try to do something about you, to, to save you, to save you. And she told me if anything happens, I should come to her. And she'll help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still living with your brother? Was your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brother was in Siberia at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your other brother. Your other brother and sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, yes. So I-- and she was the right. The same night, the same night, started, you know, started the liquidation. But most of the Jewish people somehow built some kind in the basement, hiding places, you know, with the hope that this would be help or save them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this house where my brother lived and I, so they created it in the basement was a hiding place. So we went down into this hiding place, and he stayed there without food, without water almost a week. Later, we couldn't, couldn't stay anymore. Could you hear what was going on outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard shooting, and we know what was going on outside. So one day, I decided, no use. I walked out. And most of the people were already taken away. What the Germans did, they were digging. Digging whatever you call it-- not pits, but you get--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trenches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenches, yeah. And they took away every day, the people, the Jewish people. They gathered near a gate, like a gate. And when it get a little dark, truck, German trucks with, with Ukrainian policeman together take the Jews and there they are killing the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shooting them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shooting them, yeah. And I-- when I walked out from this hiding place, then a German sergeant-- whatever he was-- caught me and started to beat me terribly on my face. [INAUDIBLE]. Blood was starting to run. My impression was that he wanted me to cry. But somehow, I couldn't cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was beating me. And then he took me to the place what-- in front-- next to the gate. And during the day, the ones they caught, the truck co-- came to take it away. And, and but at the last minute, when the truck moved in into the ghetto, moved in, the same minute, I, by instinct, ran into the ghetto, ran into, into a next house that was already empty. There were no people, into a house. And next to me was a guy with a wife and two children. And he left the wife and the two children, and followed me. I never forget that. How people could do it, you know how I say? But he did it. Situation like this, human beings don't behave like human beings, you know? So he ran after me, and it was evening. And we were in the ghetto. You and he?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are still together. He joined me, and we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there more people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was during the night, we walked out from this house, and two Ukrainian policemen met us, and they ask us to show them the place where rich people live. They were just-- you know, they just were looting, you know? So they didn't come at that time to catch me. So they let me go, and the other guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the house, the end of the ghetto, you know, there was a window and this-- and I looked over. It was very early in the morning, very early in the morning. And some of the women, the Ukrainian women, are just picking up things, you know? Looting. Looting. And I ask one of her, do you see the police, or the Germans. And she looks out from the window, and she says, they just went this way. So I jumped out from the house, and the other guy jumped after me. And we were running up-- there are hills in that city, Krzemieniec. It 's not flat, but some hills, like not big mountains, but hills. And then we ran up to hills. And one of the native Ukrainian ran-- was running after us. And this was dangerous because usually they, they immediately notify the Germans, you know? But this guy was running after us, and then we came to a place where there's nobody there. No houses. Nobody there. We sat down. I was here with all of the blood, the blood running here, still here and of course, hungry and tired. And he asked us for money. And then we told you, we don't have money. We didn't have a penny. But he was somehow a nice guy enough, that he brought us some buttermilk, whatever it is here, and a piece of bread, and bread. Just didn't expect it from him, but I thought he was a nice human being, you know? And he left us. He left us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I told the other guy, I said, I know a woman who told me that if anything happens, I should come to her. And he was a native guy. He was born there. And he said, I was born here and lived here all my life. But I don't know one person that I would-- that I could come and expect him to, to, to, help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we have to stop here.
